misconceptionSummary: Students don't see why the grid method gives the same result as column multiplication.

visuals.ruleText: Multiplication distributes over addition: a × (b + c) = a×b + a×c
visuals.subText: Partitioning a number and multiplying each part separately always works

steps.narration: The grid method works because of the distributive law. Multiplying by a split number is identical to multiplying by each part and adding the results. This is not a trick — it is a mathematical guarantee.

Picture a rectangle three units tall and two hundred and thirty-one units wide. Split the width into two hundred, thirty, and one. Each section of the rectangle is a partial product — the areas add to the total.

lines.text: Both methods apply the distributive law

steps.narration: The grid and column methods produce the same partial products — they are two presentations of the same distributive law. The column method is more compact; the grid makes the reasoning visible.

visuals.label: Distributive law: multiply each part then add

steps.narration: Six hundred and ninety-three. Partitioning and distributing is not a workaround — it is exactly what the column method compresses into a neater layout.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: thinking the grid method is less rigorous than column multiplication. Both apply the distributive law identically — the grid just makes each partial product visible.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: The grid and column methods are the same mathematics. Both split the number into parts, multiply each part, and add. The grid makes every step explicit.
